content.text: Branching and merging are the core mechanics that let teams (and AI agents) develop features, fix bugs, and experiment in isolation — then bring that work back into a shared history without overwriting each other's changes. Every serious AI-assisted development workflow depends on understanding these mechanics at the commit level, not just at the UI button level.
What it is
A
branch

in Git is a lightweight, movable pointer to a commit. When you create a branch you are not copying files — you are just naming a position in the commit graph. All subsequent commits on that branch advance the pointer forward, while other branches stay exactly where they were. This is why branching in Git is nearly instantaneous regardless of repository size.
Merging is the act of taking the diverged histories of two branches and combining them into a new commit (the
merge commit
) that has both branches as parents. Git finds the nearest
common ancestor

commit (the merge base), then applies a three-way diff: changes from the merge base to the source branch, and changes from the merge base to the target branch, are combined. Where both branches changed the same region of the same file, Git flags a
merge conflict
that a human (or agent) must resolve.
Why it matters

Without branches, every developer and every AI code-generation run would need to touch the same files in the same working tree simultaneously. Conflicts would be unresolvable because there would be no record of which edits came from which intent. Branching enforces
causal separation
: each line of work accumulates its own audit trail, and integration is a deliberate, reviewable event rather than a chaotic overwrite.

For agentic AI systems that autonomously write and commit code, branching is a safety boundary. An agent operates on a feature branch, not on main. If the agent produces broken code, the main branch is unaffected. A CI pipeline can validate the agent's branch independently, and a human or orchestrator reviews and approves the merge. This pattern — agent works on branch, CI validates, human approves merge — is the foundation of safe AI-assisted development.
Benefits
Parallel development
— multiple agents or developers work on separate branches without interfering
Isolated experimentation
— try a risky refactor on a branch; discard the branch if it fails
Reviewable integration
— pull requests make the merge event explicit and auditable
Stable main branch
— main stays deployable; no half-finished feature ever lands there directly
Emergency hotfixes
— a hotfix branch lets you patch production without shipping unfinished features
Clean history with rebase
— replaying commits linearly makes git log and git bisect effective
Trade-offs
Long-lived branches accumulate drift
— the longer a branch lives, the harder the eventual merge
Merge conflicts require manual resolution
— automated conflict resolution is unreliable for non-trivial overlaps
Rebase rewrites history
— rebasing a shared branch breaks collaborators' local copies (force push needed)
Branch proliferation
— uncollected stale branches clutter the repository and confuse agents navigating refs
Octopus merges become tangled
— merging many branches simultaneously without squashing makes bisect and blame harder
How it works

Git stores the repository as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of commit objects. Each commit records a pointer to its parent commit(s), the root tree object, and metadata (author, timestamp, message). A branch name is just a file in
.git/refs/heads/
containing the SHA-1 (or SHA-256 in newer repos) of the tip commit.
HEAD
is a symbolic ref pointing at the current branch name.
When Git performs a
three-way merge
it computes three inputs:
Base
— the most recent common ancestor commit of the two branch tips
Ours
— the current branch tip (the branch you are merging into)
Theirs
— the incoming branch tip (the branch being merged)

For each file hunk: if only ours changed from base, take ours. If only theirs changed, take theirs. If both changed identically, take either. If both changed differently, emit a conflict marker. This logic is deterministic and reproducible given the same three commits.
A
fast-forward merge

is a special case: if the target branch has no new commits since the source branch diverged, Git simply advances the target pointer — no merge commit is created. This is the cleanest outcome and is always safe.
Architecture

The diagram below shows two common patterns: a merge-commit flow (top half) where a feature branch and a hotfix branch are integrated into main via explicit merge commits, and a rebase flow (bottom half) where feature commits are replayed linearly onto main, producing new commit SHAs.
When to use it
Branch for every unit of distinct intent. In an AI-assisted codebase, that typically means:
Each agent task run
— one branch per agent invocation so its output is reviewable in isolation
Feature development
— even for a single-developer project, separate features live on separate branches
Bug fixes and hotfixes
— hotfixes branch off the current production tag, not off main, so uncommitted features are excluded
Experiments
— risky architectural changes are safe to abandon by just deleting the branch
Release staging
— a release/v1.2 branch receives only stabilisation fixes while main advances
How to implement
Core Git commands

# Create and switch to a new branch
git switch -c feature/tool-retry
# equivalent: git checkout -b feature/tool-retry

# Push branch and set upstream tracking
git push -u origin feature/tool-retry

# List local branches with last commit summary
git branch -v

# List remote-tracking branches
git branch -r

# Merge feature into main (creates a merge commit)
git switch main
git merge --no-ff feature/tool-retry
# --no-ff forces a merge commit even if fast-forward is possible;
# preserves branch topology in history for auditability

# Squash-merge: combine feature commits into one commit on main
git merge --squash feature/tool-retry
git commit -m "feat: add tool retry with exponential backoff"

# Rebase feature onto current main tip
git switch feature/tool-retry
git rebase main
# Replays each feature commit on top of main,
# creating new commits with new SHAs

# Interactive rebase: reorder, squash, edit commits
git rebase -i main

# Delete branch after merging
git branch -d feature/tool-retry          # safe (only if merged)
git push origin --delete feature/tool-retry  # remote
Automating branch creation for agent runs

"""agent_branch.py -- create a timestamped branch for each agent task run."""
import subprocess
import sys
from datetime import datetime


def git(cmd: list[str], check: bool = True) -> subprocess.CompletedProcess:
    """Run a git command and return the result."""
    return subprocess.run(
        ["git"] + cmd,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
        check=check,
    )


def create_agent_branch(task_slug: str) -> str:
    """Create a branch named agent/<slug>-<timestamp> off current HEAD.

    Returns the new branch name.
    """
    ts = datetime.utcnow().strftime("%Y%m%dT%H%M%S")
    branch = f"agent/{task_slug}-{ts}"

    # Ensure working tree is clean before branching
    status = git(["status", "--porcelain"])
    if status.stdout.strip():
        raise RuntimeError(
            "Working tree is dirty. Commit or stash changes before branching."
        )

    git(["switch", "-c", branch])
    git(["push", "-u", "origin", branch])
    print(f"Created branch: {branch}")
    return branch


def merge_agent_branch(branch: str, main: str = "main") -> None:
    """Fast-forward or merge agent branch into main after CI passes.

    Uses --no-ff to preserve branch topology for audit trail.
    """
    git(["switch", main])
    git(["pull", "--ff-only", "origin", main])  # sync with remote first
    git(["merge", "--no-ff", branch, "-m", f"Merge {branch} into {main}"])
    git(["push", "origin", main])
    git(["branch", "-d", branch])
    git(["push", "origin", "--delete", branch])
    print(f"Merged and deleted: {branch}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    b = create_agent_branch("refactor-retry-logic")
    # ... agent makes commits here ...
    # After CI passes:
    # merge_agent_branch(b)
Examples
Resolving a merge conflict programmatically

"""conflict_resolver.py -- parse and resolve simple conflict markers."""
import re
from pathlib import Path

# Conflict marker pattern produced by git merge
CONFLICT_PATTERN = re.compile(
    r"<<<<<<< .*?\n"    # our header
    r"(.*?)"            # ours
    r"=======\n"        # separator
    r"(.*?)"            # theirs
    r">>>>>>> .*?\n",   # their header
    re.DOTALL,
)


def resolve_by_strategy(
    path: Path,
    strategy: str = "ours",
) -> int:
    """Resolve all conflicts in a file using a fixed strategy.

    strategy: 'ours' | 'theirs'
    Returns the number of conflicts resolved.
    """
    text = path.read_text()
    count = len(CONFLICT_PATTERN.findall(text))
    if count == 0:
        return 0

    if strategy == "ours":
        resolved = CONFLICT_PATTERN.sub(lambda m: m.group(1), text)
    elif strategy == "theirs":
        resolved = CONFLICT_PATTERN.sub(lambda m: m.group(2), text)
    else:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown strategy: {strategy}")

    path.write_text(resolved)
    print(f"{path}: resolved {count} conflict(s) using '{strategy}'")
    return count


def find_conflicted_files(repo_root: Path) -> list[Path]:
    """Return paths of files with unresolved conflict markers."""
    conflicted = []
    for f in repo_root.rglob("*"):
        if f.is_file() and not any(part.startswith(".") for part in f.parts):
            try:
                text = f.read_text(errors="ignore")
                if "<<<<<<< " in text:
                    conflicted.append(f)
            except (PermissionError, OSError):
                pass
    return conflicted


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import subprocess

    root = Path(".")
    files = find_conflicted_files(root)
    for f in files:
        resolve_by_strategy(f, strategy="ours")
        # Stage the resolved file
        subprocess.run(["git", "add", str(f)], check=True)
    if files:
        subprocess.run(
            ["git", "commit", "-m", "resolve: accept ours for all conflicts"],
            check=True,
        )
        print("Merge commit created.")
    else:
        print("No conflicts found.")
Real-world example
A CI/CD pipeline for an AI agent-driven repository works as follows. When a developer opens a task ticket, the orchestrator:
Creates agent/task-1234-20260711T0900 from the latest main.
Runs the code-generation agent, which makes several small commits to the branch.
Pushes the branch, triggering the CI pipeline (lint, unit tests, integration tests).
If CI passes, the orchestrator opens a PR. A human reviewer approves and merges via --squash to keep main history clean.
The branch is deleted automatically by the "Delete head branch on merge" repository setting.

If CI fails, the agent re-runs on the same branch, pushes a fix commit, and CI re-triggers. No manual intervention is needed unless the agent reaches its retry limit, at which point it files a comment on the PR and waits for human input.
Common mistakes
Committing directly to main
— bypasses review; one broken commit from an agent can destabilise the entire team's working copy
Rebasing a shared branch
— rewriting commits that others have pulled creates diverged histories and requires force push
Long-lived feature branches
— branches that survive for weeks accumulate massive conflict potential; prefer short-lived PRs
Forgetting to delete merged branches
— agent tooling that lists branches to decide where to work gets confused by stale refs
Using merge when rebase is intended
— merging main back into a feature branch repeatedly creates tangled merge commit graphs
Ignoring conflict markers in automated pipelines
— agents that fail to detect unresolved markers before committing ship broken code to CI
Branching from a stale base
— an agent that does not fetch before branching may work on an hours-old snapshot of main
Best practices
Protect main with branch-protection rules
— require at least one passing CI check and one human approval before any merge
Name branches with a prefix scheme
— agent/, feature/, hotfix/, release/ make branch purpose scannable without opening each one
Rebase before opening a PR, not during review
— rebasing mid-review invalidates existing PR comments and forces reviewers to re-read
Use squash-merge for agent branches
— agent commits are often noisy work-in-progress; squashing keeps main history readable
Fetch before every new branch
— git fetch origin && git switch -c feature/X origin/main ensures you start from the real latest tip
Write meaningful commit messages on agent branches
— the PR squash message is what humans read; instruct the agent to summarise its work in the final commit
Automate stale branch cleanup
— a weekly cron job that deletes branches merged more than 7 days ago prevents namespace pollution
Performance & scalability

Git's branch model is extremely lightweight. Creating a branch writes exactly one file (40 bytes for a SHA-1 ref). Switching branches rewrites the working tree in time proportional only to files that differ between the two branch tips — not to total repository size. This makes it practical for agents to create a new branch per task, even in repositories with millions of files, as long as the changed file set is small.

The cost is in merging and conflict detection. Three-way merge complexity scales with the number of changed hunks between the two branches, not with total repository size. For very large monorepos (millions of lines, hundreds of concurrent branches), teams use:
Merge queues
— GitHub's Merge Queue serialises PRs to prevent the 'you passed CI but the merged result did not' problem
Sparse checkout
— agents that only touch a subtree clone only that subtree, making switch operations near-instant
Shallow clones
— git clone --depth 1 is sufficient for agents that only need to read recent history
Worktrees
— git worktree add lets multiple agent processes work on different branches in parallel without cloning twice
Tooling & ecosystem
GitHub / GitLab / Bitbucket
— hosted Git with pull request / merge request workflows, branch protection rules, and merge queues
GitHub CLI (gh)
— agents can create PRs and merge branches via gh pr create and gh pr merge without using the web UI
GitPython
— Python library that wraps git commands, useful for agents that need programmatic repository manipulation
Gitoxide
— high-performance Rust implementation of Git; useful for tooling that needs fast history traversal at scale
pre-commit / Lefthook
— hook frameworks that enforce lint and tests before every commit, catching agent-generated issues early
delta / diff-so-fancy
— enhanced diff pagers that make conflict review and branch comparison more readable
Comparison with alternatives
The table compares the three main strategies for integrating branches:
Strategy
History shape
Rewrites SHAs
Best for
Watch out for
Merge (--no-ff)
Non-linear; branch topology preserved
No
Long-lived release branches where exact integration point matters
Many merge commits clutter git log
Squash merge
Linear; feature work collapsed to one commit
Yes (new SHA)
Agent branches with noisy intermediate commits
Loses per-commit authorship in main history
Rebase + fast-forward
Linear; commits replayed on main tip
Yes (new SHA)
Solo developer or CI-driven agent needing clean bisectable history
Never rebase branches others have checked out
Trunk-based (no feature branches)
Strictly linear
N/A
High-velocity teams with full CI coverage and feature flags
Every commit must be deployable; hard constraint for WIP agent runs
Security & reliability
Protecting main from agent mistakes
Require status checks
— no merge unless CI passes (test, lint, security scan)
Require pull request reviews
— at least one human must approve before merging agent-generated code
Restrict push access to main
— the agent's CI token should only have permission to push to its own namespace (agent/*)
Enable signed commits
— GPG or SSH commit signing lets you verify which commits came from a verified agent identity versus an attacker
Audit the merge commit message
— include the task ID, agent version, and CI run URL so post-incident analysis can trace every change to its origin
Secret scanning on push
— agents that handle API keys might accidentally commit them; GitHub's secret scanning blocks this at push time
Reliability: never lose work
Git's content-addressed storage (every object addressed by its SHA) means data is never silently corrupted — a bad write produces a different SHA, which fails verification. The reflog (stored in
.git/logs/
) records every movement of every branch tip for 90 days by default, making any accidental deletion recoverable via
git reflog
followed by
git switch -c recovered <sha>
. Agents should push branches to origin frequently so work is not lost if the local container is recycled.
Mental model

Think of the commit graph as an immutable river delta. Each commit is a rock dropped into the water, permanently placed. A branch name is a floating buoy anchored to the most recent rock on its current. When you commit, the buoy moves to the new rock; all older rocks stay exactly where they are.

Merging is dropping a new rock that has two anchor chains — one to the last rock on the target stream, one to the last rock on the source stream. The two streams join but both their histories remain intact upstream of the junction.

Rebasing lifts the rocks off the source stream and places copies of them at the end of the target stream. The originals are still there (in the reflog) but the buoy now points at the copies. The river looks linear, but the original rocks exist for 90 days in case you need them.
Key takeaways
A branch is a pointer, not a copy
— creating one is free; the DAG stores the actual history
Three-way merge is deterministic
— given the same three commits it always produces the same result or the same conflicts
Rebase rewrites SHAs
— never rebase branches that others have already pulled
Agent branches should be short-lived
— create, commit, push, CI, merge, delete within one task cycle
Branch protection is the safety harness for AI-generated code
— no agent should ever push directly to a protected branch
git reflog is your undo button
— almost any Git mistake is recoverable within 90 days via the reflog
Choose the integration strategy by branch purpose
— squash for agent branches, rebase for solo work, merge --no-ff for long-lived release branches
Fetch before branching
— stale base commits mean more conflicts later; always start from the latest remote state
